posts.name: Vamsi Kaipa
posts.cooked:
Hello All,
I am mainly interested in Burgundy. Which one should I subscribe to and why?

Thanks in advance.

posts.name: Eric Lundblad
posts.cooked:
Both, and vinous.com as well. Figure out which one matches your palate the best…stick with them and drop the others. Getting excellent advice that’s well matched to your preferences is invaluable.

There are other ways of doing this…such as finding a retailer in your area with someone with a sharp palate that you communicate well with is also a great way. Only problem here is, unless they’re the owner, if they’re really good they’re likely to move on to another job at some point (with a distributor for example). Still a very good avenue to pursue tho.

link_counts.title: Antonio Galloni presents Vinous | Explore All Things Wine

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked: Burghound, much more comprehensive than Gilman and featuring not just the top Domaines, but also many not super well known Domaines. Also, Gilman is much about personal taste while Meadows seems more balanced in that regard.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: different perspectives are always helpful. Instead of Galloni, Tanzer.

posts.name: Gary York
posts.cooked: You get both with Vinous.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
You might also want to check out Claude Kolm’s Fine Wine Review blog, too. He focuses (though not exclusively) on Burgundy, and has been visiting the region since the 1980s.

Full disclosure: Claude is an old friend.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
StevenB:

Burghound, much more comprehensive than Gilman and featuring not just the top Domaines, but also many not super well known Domaines. Also, Gilman is much about personal taste while Meadows seems more balanced in that regard.

This. Before subscribing to Gilman, make sure your palate is aligned with his. Mine tends to be so I have found John’s newsletter to be very helpful over the years.

Burghound comes out 4 times a year. Issue one, Cotes du Nuits reds, issue two, Cotes du Beaune reds, issues three Cotes du Beaune whites and issue four Chablis and Macon. A few articles near the end of the issue on Champagne or US pinot, but mostly all burgs all the time. Very comprehensive. Allen tries to be objective and not impose his palate on you.

A View to a Cellar is not just about Burgundy or even primarily about Burgundy, although that is a main focus. But, so are German wines, Piedmont wines, Champagne and anything else coming from a producer John admires as making traditionally styled wines. There are six issues a year. John does not try to be objective. He gives you HIS view of what the wine tastes like and his ratings are based on what he thinks wines SHOULD taste like.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
bang for the buck, burghound. as it relates to burgundy. but as for a general wine journal that skews towards burgundy, you’ll get more depth and POV from gilman.

but i don’t think there’s any significant difference as far as recommendations and guidance goes between gilman and meadows. in fact, you need to search very hard to see any variations.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked: IMO, the Burgundy ratings are mostly really hierarchical, especially with Burghound. BTW, if a critic trashes a famous domaine, he will be banned. I personally like Vinous.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
What does “hierarchical” mean…Kevin.?
John…I had no idea Claude was still “around”… is his stuff free?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

What does “hierarchical” mean…Kevin.?

I assume he means the scores correspond to the vineyards’ pecking order.

Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

John…I had no idea Claude was still “around”… is his stuff free?

It is now. You can see it all at the link above.

posts.name: Kevin_S_hin
posts.cooked:
John Morris:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

What does “hierarchical” mean…Kevin.?

I assume he means the scores correspond to the vineyards’ pecking order.

Exactly.

Village +2 = Lavaux st Jacques +2 = Clos St. Jacques + 2 = Chambertin.

Most of the critics completely missed the 04 and a critic wrote that I and Bill Nanson have no clue for suggesting that the 11s showed GM.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
For that and other reasons (the Rudy-vouching debacle), I stopped all such subscriptions.

For domaine descriptions, they are ok, though Morris’ book is best.
Otherwise, I think it’s a better investment to go to tastings of wines available in your area. Form your own tastes and buy only what you have tasted…or at least from producers you have tasted over and over again, and ,therefore, “know”.

That’s how I bought…only what I tasted. Then, you can only blame yourself for mistakes.

None of these guys found the 2004 problem and some of them never recognized it any wines they tasted. What’s their value then?

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: [quote="Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow]None of these guys found the 2004 problem and some of them never recognized it any wines they tasted. What’s their value then?[/quote]I pointed out in a prior thread that Tanzer had identified the green in his early tastings.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked: yes…i vaguely remember that…though his reviews were later than BH’s…when the issue was widely known. Do you have a link? I forget the details…and would like to credit Tanzer, as I always found his notes the most helpful of anyone’s…though more diverse

posts.name: Robert Love
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

yes…i vaguely remember that…though his reviews were later than BH’s…when the issue was widely known. Do you have a link? I forget the details…and would like to credit Tanzer, as I always found his notes the most helpful of anyone’s…though more diverse

I would also like to see a link to some examples of Tanzer finding early 2004s green. I’ve always enjoyed his TNs.

Point of fact, I don’t see a lot of references to the greenness of 2004s in many professional TNs, even those done today. I’ve always found that weird. In general I’d like to see professional critics’ notes take a more nuanced understanding of the wine. For example, few critics—Allen Meadows of Burghound is a rare counterexample—directly state that a wine is reduced or that notes derive from reduction.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
It shows the folly of relying on others’ notes in general…when we all have different sensitivities…genetically and well as differentvocabulary.

That’s why I suggest spending the money on tasting wines instead…

One “professional” critic… mentioned above can’t even taste the flaw, as he has admitted. Another one vouched for Rudy’s rudimentary fakes as authentic (and hid that he was compensated for his opinions by those selling them)…one retired critic created such superficial notes that he was parodied for repeating them so frequently.

Then, some such tasters sub out their tastings…to people…you can’t calibrate.

And, all except Gilman only taste in barrel…and very rarely on any evolutionary improvement. Though that is the best, most economic (ie, free) and efficient way to taste widely…it is just a snapshot stage…and…some issues (like the GM of 2004) show up thereafter anyway.

No good answer…except to buy only what you can taste yourself, IMO…and that’s not too practical either.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

[quote="Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow]None of these guys found the 2004 problem and some of them never recognized it any wines they tasted. What’s their value then?

I pointed out in a prior thread that Tanzer had identified the green in his early tastings.[/quote][/quote][/quote][/quote]

My understanding from talking to Gilman a couple of years ago is that he still thinks the 2004s will turn out well if you give them time.

Also, I was at a tasting Allen did in DC with 2004s where he liked wines I thought were really green - tasting was probably in somewhere around 2007-2009 time frame.

posts.name: Tracy  Reasoner
posts.cooked:
2nd on Morris hard bound Book and the ibook version.

2nd on Tanzer and Vinous!

you certainly cannot lose with both choices

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I am a Gilman fan. While he does not attempt to taste ‘objectively’, a dubious proposition at best to my mind (though one better not debated here), he does taste very consistently. He also tries to give ageworthy, traditionally-style wines their due, which is helpful if those are wines you appreciate.

But why not ask for trial issues of each and see what you find most useful?
